title: AI-Assisted Security Review of a Pull Request
description: You are a mid-level backend developer at a fintech startup. A colleague has submitted a pull request that adds a new REST API endpoint for processing user payment refunds. You are using an AI assistant to help review the code for security vulnerabilities before approving the PR. The codebase is a Node.js/Express application with a PostgreSQL database.
learningObjectives: Learn to provide sufficient codebase context when asking AI to review code
Develop critical evaluation skills for AI-generated security assessments
Practice verifying AI findings against actual code behavior
Understand the limitations of AI in detecting complex security vulnerabilities
Build the habit of asking targeted follow-up questions rather than accepting surface-level reviews

setup.role: Mid-level backend developer at FinFlow, a fintech startup handling payment processing
setup.task: Review a pull request that adds a /api/v2/refunds endpoint. Use the AI assistant to identify security vulnerabilities, but critically evaluate every finding and probe for issues the AI might miss.
constraints: The PR contains 4 files: a route handler, a service layer, a database query module, and a test file
The application handles real financial transactions and PCI compliance is required
You must not approve the PR until all critical and high-severity issues are resolved
You have access to the existing codebase's authentication and authorization middleware
The AI does not have access to the full codebase — only what you paste into the conversation
availableTools: The pull request diff (provided as context)

files.name: routes/refunds.js
knownIssues: Missing rate limiting on the refund endpoint
No input validation on the refund amount field
SQL query uses string interpolation instead of parameterized queries

files.name: services/refundService.js
knownIssues: No check that the requesting user owns the original transaction
Refund amount can exceed original transaction amount
No idempotency key to prevent duplicate refunds

files.name: db/refundQueries.js
knownIssues: Raw SQL with string concatenation (SQL injection vulnerability)
No transaction wrapping for the multi-step refund process
Database credentials logged in debug mode

files.name: tests/refunds.test.js
knownIssues: No tests for authorization edge cases
No tests for negative or zero refund amounts

existingMiddleware.auth: JWT-based authentication with role-based access control
existingMiddleware.logging: Winston logger with PII redaction in production mode

good: Provides the full PR diff to the AI along with relevant context about the existing codebase
Asks the AI to focus specifically on security vulnerabilities, not just code style
Verifies each AI finding by cross-referencing with the actual code
Asks follow-up questions about edge cases the AI did not mention (e.g., race conditions, TOCTOU)
Questions the AI when it reports 'no issues found' in a file that clearly has problems
Asks about authorization checks — can any authenticated user trigger a refund for any transaction?
Probes for business logic vulnerabilities, not just technical ones
Asks the AI about the absence of idempotency handling
Checks whether the AI identified the SQL injection vulnerability in the query module
Does not approve the PR based solely on the AI's assessment
bad: Pastes the code and asks 'is this code good?' without specifying what to look for
Accepts an 'LGTM' or 'no major issues' response without probing further
Does not provide context about the existing auth middleware, leading the AI to miss integration issues
Trusts the AI's claim that parameterized queries are used when the code actually uses string interpolation
Ignores the test file, assuming AI will catch issues there automatically
Does not ask about business logic (e.g., refund exceeding original amount)
Approves the PR after a single round of AI review without follow-up questions
Fails to notice that the AI missed the SQL injection vulnerability
Does not consider PCI compliance implications

evaluationCriteria.description: Did the user provide sufficient code context, including the diff and relevant existing codebase details?
Did the user direct the AI to focus on security, authorization, and business logic rather than generic review?
Did the user verify AI findings against the actual code and catch cases where the AI was wrong or incomplete?
Did the user ask targeted follow-up questions to probe for vulnerabilities the AI missed?
Were the major known vulnerabilities (SQL injection, missing authorization, no rate limiting, etc.) identified by the end of the session?
Did the user make an appropriate approve/reject decision based on a thorough review, not just the AI's summary?
